title: Photon-energy-programmable subnanometric electron birth-site control
authors: Hirofumi Yanagisawa, Abhisek Sinha, Ravi Kumar, Neill Lambert, Hirotaka Kitoh-Nishioka
abstract: Optical control of electron-generation sites has broadly enabled ultrafast nanoscale imaging, spectroscopy, and functional control. Existing approaches achieve nanoscale site selectivity by shaping localised optical fields around nanostructures, thereby limiting independent site selectivity within the same local-field hotspot. Here, using a single-molecule electron emitter, we show that site selectivity can instead be encoded in the electronic excitation pathway, enabling subnanometric control of electron birth sites within the same local-field hotspot. By tuning the photon energy, we selectively access molecular states of different spatial symmetry and reversibly switch the electron birth site between distinct locations in the same emitter, with the change read out directly in the far-field emission pattern. The switching depends on photon energy alone and is absent under variations in intensity or polarisation. Our results establish optical birth-site selectivity that is not dictated by the local-field distribution, opening a route to electron birth-site control through the electronic excitation pathway.
summary: This paper shows that the site of electron emission from a single molecule can be controlled at a subnanometer scale simply by changing the energy of the incoming photons. By tuning the photon energy, researchers can switch between different molecular orbitals, effectively moving the electron 'birth site' without changing the optical field distribution. This provides a new mechanism for high-resolution nanoscale imaging and control.
main_problem: Conventional optical control of electron-generation sites is limited by the need to physically shape local-field hotspots, making it impossible to independently select different substructures within the same field distribution.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated that electron birth-site control can be achieved at a subnanometric scale by tuning photon energy to selectively access molecular orbitals of different spatial symmetries, independent of the local-field profile.
method: The study combines experimental electron emission measurements from single-molecule emitters with theoretical modeling using DFT (GAMESS/VASP) and a three-step emission model involving orbital-resolved weighting and field propagation.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of single-molecule electron emitters (C60 multilayers) at the apex of a metallic tungsten nanotip under ultrahigh vacuum and a strong DC field.
key_observables: Far-field electron emission patterns (e.g., ring-like vs. two-lobed structures) and normalized lobe separation (d/d0).
important_parameters: Photon energies of 2.99 eV and 3.18 eV; subnanometric spatial resolution (~0.3 nm); DC field of 4.5 V/nm; electron yield < 0.05 per pulse.
main_assumptions: The model assumes an incoherent sum of orbital contributions and utilizes a normal-incidence approximation for the decay constant of the post-barrier wavefunction.
figures_summary: Fig 1 compares field-based vs. energy-dependent control; Fig 2 shows experimental reversible switching of emission patterns and simulation overlays; Fig 3 demonstrates the invariance of patterns to polarization and power; Fig 4 shows the asymmetric energy-supply function.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the limitation of local-field control, presents the experimental demonstration of energy-dependent switching, provides a theoretical model for symmetry-selective excitation, and validates the mechanism through polarization and power-dependence tests.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and molecular physics as it demonstrates a new way to manipulate quantum-scale electron pathways using the internal electronic structure (symmetry) of a molecule rather than external field shaping.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 | Concept of photon-energy-dependent subnanometric control of electron-emission
Fig. 2 | Reversible photon-energy-dependent switching of molecular emission patterns.
Fig. 3 | Emission-pattern invariance under polarisation and power variations.
Fig. 4 | Symmetry-resolved emission channels from the molecular scale to the far field.
